MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 6 – “P” for ProductPart 4 - Branding strategy: Building strong brands
Chapter 7 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

Part 4 - Branding strategy: Building strong brands

Branding strategy: Building strong brands (1 of 9)
Brand equity
Brand equity is the differential effect that knowing the brand name has on customer response to the product or its marketing. It can be either positive or negative.
One measure of brand equity uses four consumer perception dimensions:
differentiation (what makes the brand stand out)
relevance (how consumers feel the brand meets their needs)
knowledge (how much consumers know about the brand)
esteem (how highly consumers regard and respect the brand).

Branding strategy: Building strong brands (3 of 9)
Building strong brands
Major brand strategy decisions involve:
brand positioning
brand name selection
brand sponsorship
brand development.
Q: Thinking about two or three of your favourite brand names, what are the characteristics of these brand names that make them stand out in your mind?

Figure 7.6
Major brand strategy decisions

Branding strategy: Building strong brands (5 of 9)
Building strong brands (cont.)
Brand positioning
Marketers need to position their brands clearly in target consumers’ minds.
They can position brands at any of three levels, based on:
product attributes (e.g. quality, selection, style)
association with a desirable benefit (e.g. safety)
beliefs and values that connect with and engage consumers.

Branding strategy: Building strong brands (6 of 9)
Building strong brands (cont.)
Brand name selection
A good name can add greatly to a product’s success. Finding the best brand name begins with a careful review of the product, its benefits, the target market and proposed marketing strategies.
Some of the desirable qualities for a brand name are that it:
relates to its benefits and qualities
is easy to pronounce, recognise and remember
is extendable
can be protected.
Q: What are some other desirable qualities for a brand name?

Branding strategy: Building strong brands (7 of 9)
Building strong brands (cont.)
Brand sponsorship
A manufacturer has four brand sponsorship options:
national brand (manufacturer’s brand) –  where a manufacturer sells its output under its own brand names
a store brand (private brand) – a brand created and owned by a reseller of a product or service
licensed brand – where a company licenses names or symbols previously created by other manufacturers, or names of well-known celebrities
co-brand – where two established brand names of different companies are used on the same product.

Branding strategy: Building strong brands (8 of 9)
Building strong brands (cont.)
Brand development
Brands can be developed in four main ways:
Line extensions refer to extending an existing brand name to new forms, colours, sizes, ingredients or flavours of an existing product category.
Brand extensions refer to extending an existing brand name to new product categories.
Multibrands refers to marketing many different brands in a given product category.
New brand names arise to update a waning brand name, or are created when a company enters a new product category.

Branding strategy: Building strong brands (9 of 9)
Building strong brands (cont.)
Managing brands
Companies must manage their brands carefully.
Companies need to periodically audit their brands’ strengths and weaknesses.
Q: Give examples of companies that you think manage their brands effectively.

Branding strategy: Building strong brands
Q: Thinking about two or three of your favourite brand names, what are the characteristics of these brand names that make them stand out in your mind? Some of the desirable qualities for a brand name are that it:
relates to its benefits and qualities
is easy to pronounce, recognise and remember
is extendable
can be protected.
A: Ajax Spray N’ Wipe
Benefits and qualities – yes, sounds like it will be easy to use, and quick.
Easy to pronounce – yes, simple English words,
Recognise – Ajax is distinctive and Spray N’ Wipe is easy to recognize.
Easy to remember – yes.
Extendable – yes, could be used as a name for many cleaning products.
Protected – Ajax is a protected brand name, but Spray N’ Wipe are common English words so might not be legally protected without “Ajax”.

Branding strategy: Building strong brands
Q: What are the characteristics of these brand names that make them stand out in your mind?
A: Kodak
Benefits and qualities – NO, but over time Kodak, working with advertising agency J. Walter Thompson, imbued this meaningless* name with brand associations that created brand equity.
Easy to pronounce – yes, in any language. *ALSO has no rude or unfortunate meanings in any language.
Recognise – distinctive.
Easy to remember – yes.
Extendable – yes, could be used as a name for many products.
Protected – yes because it is non-meaningful and unique.

Branding strategy: Building strong brands
Q: What are some other desirable qualities for a brand name?
A: sounds nice, can be placed in many different positions including products, billboards, TV commercials etc., no rude or unfortunate meanings in any language.

Branding strategy: Building strong brands marketing
A: In my opinion – Toyota. They consistently deliver value-for-money vehicles which consistently perform and deliver satisfaction over many years.
Their sales and service and dealer premises are always effective and create ongoing customer satisfaction and loyal customers – people keep coming back to buy their new Toyota.
Q: Give examples of companies that you think manage their brands effectively.

Branding strategy: Building strong brands marketing
Q: Give examples of companies that you think manage their brands effectively.
One measure of brand equity uses four consumer perception dimensions:
differentiation (what makes the brand stand out) – I think Toyota stands out for their consistency – durable, reliable vehicles.
relevance (how consumers feel the brand meets their needs) – most customers want economy in terms of the total cost of ownership – purchase, running costs and resale value – and they want peace-of-mind which the vehicle’s reliability provides for them.
knowledge (how much consumers know about the brand) – over the decades customers come to know a lot about Toyota.
esteem (how highly consumers regard and respect the brand). People respect Toyota for the consistent delivery of durability and reliability (even if they think the vehicles might be a little on the dull side).
